cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
hints: private limits access to the type; internal is the default.
The 'init' method should be 'internal' so it can be called from outside the struct but within the same module.
Use 'self.code = code' to initialize the private property.
lesson:
Lesson: Access Control
Access control restricts access to parts of your code from code in other source files and modules.

Why Use Access Control?
1. Encapsulation: Hide implementation details that shouldn't be exposed.
2. Safety: Prevent external code from modifying internal state in unexpected ways.
3. API Design: Clearly define which parts of your code are meant for public use.

Access Levels:
- 'private': The most restrictive. Use it for state that should only be touched by the current struct or class.
- 'internal': The default. Great for modular code within a single project.
- 'public': Use this when building a library or framework.
- 'open': Like public, but also allows subclasses and overrides outside the module.

Example:
    struct Vault {
        private var secretCode: Int
        private(set) var isOpen: Bool = false
        init(code: Int) { self.secretCode = code }
    }

Best Practices:
- Start with the most restrictive level ('private') and only open it up as needed.
- Use 'private(set)' for state that should be visible but not modifiable by others.
title: Access Control 1

cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
hints: public exposes types outside the module; open allows subclassing.
A class marked 'open' can be inherited from by code outside its module.
A struct marked 'public' can be used by code outside its module, but structs cannot be inherited.

title: Access Control 2

cheatsheet:
Error Handling
- Defining Errors: Use an enum that conforms to 'Error'.
- Throwing: Use 'throw MyError.something' inside a function marked 'throws'.
- Handling:
  - do { try function() } catch { ... }
  - try?: Returns an optional, nil if an error was thrown.
  - try!: Disables error propagation; crashes if an error is thrown.
- Rethrows: Used when a function takes a throwing closure and propagates its errors.
description: Use errors in a protocol-based API
hints: Throw errors when constraints are not met.
The 'heat' function in the protocol already includes 'throws', so your implementation must also include it.
Use 'do { try ... } catch { ... }' to handle the error thrown by the 'heat' function.
title: Error Integration

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
hints: Generics let you reuse logic across types.
The function signature should look like 'func swapPair<A, B>(_ first: A, _ second: B) -> (B, A)'.
Return a tuple where the second parameter is first and the first parameter is second.
lesson:
Lesson: Generics
Generics allow you to write code that avoids duplication by working with 'any' type, while still maintaining full type safety.

Key Concepts:
1. Type Parameters: Placed in angle brackets (e.g., <T>). You can use any name, but 'T', 'U', and 'V' are common.
2. Generic Types: Classes, structs, and enums can all be generic.
3. Type Constraints: Specify that a type parameter must inherit from a specific class or conform to a particular protocol.
4. Reusability: Write one function to sort arrays, regardless of whether they contain Ints, Strings, or custom objects.

Example:
    struct Box<T> {
        var content: T
    }
    let intBox = Box(content: 5)
    let stringBox = Box(content: "Forge")

Best Practices:
- Use descriptive names for type parameters if they have a specific meaning (e.g., <Element>, <Key, Value>).
- Use generics to keep your code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself).
title: Generic Function

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
hints: Generic types store values of a type parameter.
Define the struct as 'struct Box<T> { ... }'.
When you create an instance like 'Box(value: 7)', Swift infers that 'T' is 'Int'.

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Add a constraint to a generic
hints: Constraints allow you to use protocol requirements on T.
Constrain 'T' to 'Comparable' so you can use the '>=' operator.
The signature should be 'func maxValue<T: Comparable>(_ first: T, _ second: T) -> T'.
title: Type Constraints

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
hints: associatedtype lets protocols describe generic placeholders.
In the protocol, declare 'associatedtype Item'.
In the conforming struct, you don't need to explicitly name the type if it can be inferred from the 'items' property.
title: Associated Types

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Add a where clause to constrain generics
hints: where adds extra constraints for a generic extension.
Extend Array like this: 'extension Array where Element: Equatable { ... }'.
Inside the extension, use 'allSatisfy { $0 == first }' to check if all elements match the first one.
title: Where Clauses

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
hints: Constrain the shared behavior so it applies only to types that support equality checks.
Use 'extension Describable where Self: Equatable { ... }'.
The 'description()' method can then use the 'name' property from the 'Describable' protocol.
lesson:
Lesson: Extensions
Extensions add new functionality to an existing type, even if you don't have access to the original source code.

What Extensions Can Do:
1. Add Methods: Group related utility functions outside the main type definition.
2. Computed Properties: Add derived state without adding stored data.
3. Protocol Conformance: Keep your code organized by putting protocol requirements in an extension.
4. Default Implementations: Use protocol extensions to provide 'free' functionality to any type that conforms.

Example:
    extension Int {
        var isEven: Bool { return self % 2 == 0 }
    }
    print(4.isEven) // true

Best Practices:
- Use extensions to break up large types into logical blocks.
- Don't use extensions to hide complexity; use them to organize it.
title: Constrained Extension

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
hints: Conditional conformance lives in an extension with a type constraint.
Use 'extension Crate: Equatable where T: Equatable { }'.
Because 'T' is 'Equatable', Swift can automatically synthesize the '==' implementation for 'Crate' if its properties are also 'Equatable'.

title: Conditional Conformance

hints: Use protocols to define behavior and extensions for defaults.
The protocol 'StatusProviding' should require a 'status' property.
Add a default 'report()' method in an extension of 'StatusProviding' that prints the 'status'.
title: Model Layer Exercise

cheatsheet:
Memory Management (ARC)
- ARC: Automatic Reference Counting manages memory for class instances.
- Strong Reference: The default. Keeps the instance alive as long as the reference exists.
- Weak Reference: Does not keep the instance alive. Automatically becomes 'nil' when the instance is released. Must be 'var' and optional.
- Reference Cycle: Occurs when two instances hold strong references to each other, preventing them from ever being released.
- [weak self]: Used in closure capture lists to avoid reference cycles.
hints: Weak references prevent strong reference cycles.
Declare the reference in 'Operator' as 'weak var forge: Forge?'.
Because it's weak, it must be an optional and declared with 'var'.
lesson:
Lesson: Memory Management
Swift uses Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) to track and manage your app's memory usage.

How ARC Works:
1. Every time you create a class instance, ARC allocates a chunk of memory.
2. ARC tracks how many properties, constants, and variables are currently referring to each class instance.
3. When the reference count drops to zero, the instance is deallocated.

Reference Cycles:
Cycles are "memory leaks". They happen most often with:
- Parent-child relationships (e.g., a Department has many Employees, and each Employee knows their Department).
- Closures that capture 'self' while 'self' also holds a reference to the closure.

The Solution:
- Use 'weak' for one side of a parent-child relationship.
- Use '[weak self]' in closures.

Best Practices:
- Most references should be strong. Only use 'weak' when you identify a potential cycle.
- Structs and enums are value types and don't use ARC.
title: ARC Safety

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
hints: Structs are copied on assignment.
Assigning 'original' to 'copy' creates a second, separate instance of the Plate.
Changing 'copy.thickness' will not affect 'original.thickness'.
lesson:
Lesson: Structs
Structs are the building blocks of most Swift programs. They are 'value types', meaning they are copied when passed around.

Core Concepts:
1. Definition: Use the 'struct' keyword. Properties are variables or constants defined inside.
2. Memberwise Initializer: Swift automatically provides an initializer based on your properties.
3. Value Semantics: When you assign a struct to a new variable, you get a copy. Changes to the copy don't affect the original.
4. Mutability: If you want to change a struct's properties, the instance itself must be declared with 'var'.

Example:
    struct ForgeItem {
        var name: String
        var weight: Int
    }
    var item = ForgeItem(name: "Iron", weight: 5)
    item.weight = 10 // Only works because 'item' is var

Best Practices:
- Use structs for simple data models.
- Favor immutability (let) whenever possible.
solution:
copy.thickness = 5
print(original.thickness)
print(copy.thickness)
title: Struct Copy Drill

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
hints: Mutating methods can change stored properties.
Prefix your function definition with the 'mutating' keyword.
The 'increment' function should add 1 to the 'value' property.

title: Mutating Counter

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
hints: Set should convert pounds back to kg.
The 'get' block should return 'kg * 2'.
The 'set' block should update 'kg' using 'newValue / 2'.
title: Computed Conversion

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
expectedOutput:
Will set to 3
Did set from 1
hints: Use newValue and oldValue in observers.
Inside 'willSet', 'newValue' refers to the incoming value.
Inside 'didSet', 'oldValue' refers to the value that was just replaced.
title: Observer Echo

cheatsheet:
Classes (Reference Types)
- Definition: class Name { var property: Type; init(...) { ... } }
- Reference Semantics: Multiple variables can point to the same instance
- Identity: Use === to check if two variables point to the same instance
- Initializers: Unlike structs, classes DO NOT get a memberwise initializer automatically
- Deinit: A deinitializer runs when the last reference to an instance is released
- Inheritance: Classes can inherit from other classes (subclassing)
hints: Classes share a single instance across references.
Since 'primary' and 'secondary' point to the same object, 'primary.mode' will reflect the change made via 'secondary'.
This is 'Reference Semantics' – you are passing a reference to the same object in memory.
lesson:
Lesson: Classes
Classes are 'reference types'. When you assign a class instance to a new variable, both variables point to the same object in memory.

Key Differences from Structs:
1. Reference Semantics: Changing a property through one reference affects all other references to that same instance.
2. Mandatory Initializers: You must provide an 'init' method if your properties don't have default values.
3. Identity vs. Equality: Use '==' for value equality and '===' for reference identity.
4. Lifetime: Classes use Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) to determine when they should be destroyed.

Example:
    class Bellows {
        var power: Int
        init(power: Int) { self.power = power }
    }
    let p1 = Bellows(power: 1)
    let p2 = p1
    p2.power = 10 // p1.power is now also 10

When to Use:
- Use classes when you need shared mutable state or identity.
- Use classes for inheritance or when you need to perform cleanup on deinit.
solution:
secondary.mode = "Active"
print(primary.mode)
title: Class Shared State

cheatsheet:
Properties

- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
hints: Lazy properties are initialized on first access.
The property must be declared with 'lazy var'.
Use string interpolation to include the 'id' in the 'title'.
title: Lazy Builder

hints: Protocol extensions can provide default methods.
The extension should be defined as 'extension Named { ... }'.
Inside the default implementation, you can access the 'name' property defined by the protocol.
title: Default Label

description: Use a protocol as a parameter
hints: Accept any type that conforms to the protocol, not a concrete type.
The parameter 'source' in your function should be of type 'HeatSource'.
Because the function accepts the protocol, it can access any properties defined in that protocol, such as 'heat'.
title: Protocol Parameter

hints: Require both capabilities in the parameter type.
The parameter 'vent' in your function should be of type 'Fueling & Venting'.
This composition ensures that any object passed into the function implements both protocols.
title: Composition Drill

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Add a method with an extension
hints: Extensions can add methods to existing types.
Extend the 'Int' type directly: 'extension Int { ... }'.
Inside the extension, 'self' refers to the integer value itself.

    }
    let intBox = Box(content: 5)
    let stringBox = Box(content: "Forge")

Best Practices:
- Use descriptive names for type parameters if they have a specific meaning (e.g., <Element>, <Key, Value>).
- Use generics to keep your code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself).
title: Associated Storage

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
hints: Compare each element to the first.
The extension should be defined as 'extension Array where Element: Equatable { ... }'.
Use 'allSatisfy { $0 == first }' to check for equality across the whole array.
lesson:
Lesson: Generics
Generics allow you to write code that avoids duplication by working with 'any' type, while still maintaining full type safety.

Key Concepts:
1. Type Parameters: Placed in angle brackets (e.g., <T>). You can use any name, but 'T', 'U', and 'V' are common.
2. Generic Types: Classes, structs, and enums can all be generic.
3. Type Constraints: Specify that a type parameter must inherit from a specific class or conform to a particular protocol.
4. Reusability: Write one function to sort arrays, regardless of whether they contain Ints, Strings, or custom objects.

Example:
    struct Box<T> {
        var content: T
    }
    let intBox = Box(content: 5)
    let stringBox = Box(content: "Forge")

Best Practices:
- Use descriptive names for type parameters if they have a specific meaning (e.g., <Element>, <Key, Value>).
- Use generics to keep your code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself).
title: Where Clause

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.

hints: Add the constraint in an extension so conformance is conditional.
Use 'extension Wrapper: Equatable where T: Equatable { }'.
Because 'T' is 'Equatable', Swift can automatically synthesize the '==' implementation for 'Wrapper' if its properties are also 'Equatable'.

cheatsheet:
Memory Management (ARC)
- ARC: Automatic Reference Counting manages memory for class instances.
- Strong Reference: The default. Keeps the instance alive as long as the reference exists.
- Weak Reference: Does not keep the instance alive. Automatically becomes 'nil' when the instance is released. Must be 'var' and optional.
- Reference Cycle: Occurs when two instances hold strong references to each other, preventing them from ever being released.
- [weak self]: Used in closure capture lists to avoid reference cycles.
description: Use weak in a closure capture list
hints: Use a weak capture to avoid a cycle, then safely access the instance.
The closure should start with '[weak self] in'.
Because 'self' is now weak, it is optional inside the closure. Use 'if let self = self' to safely unwrap it.
lesson:
Lesson: Memory Management
Swift uses Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) to track and manage your app's memory usage.

How ARC Works:
1. Every time you create a class instance, ARC allocates a chunk of memory.
2. ARC tracks how many properties, constants, and variables are currently referring to each class instance.
3. When the reference count drops to zero, the instance is deallocated.

Reference Cycles:
Cycles are "memory leaks". They happen most often with:
- Parent-child relationships (e.g., a Department has many Employees, and each Employee knows their Department).
- Closures that capture 'self' while 'self' also holds a reference to the closure.

The Solution:
- Use 'weak' for one side of a parent-child relationship.
- Use '[weak self]' in closures.

Best Practices:
- Most references should be strong. Only use 'weak' when you identify a potential cycle.
- Structs and enums are value types and don't use ARC.
title: Weak Capture

cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
hints: internal is the default access level.
private(set) allows reads but prevents external mutation.
fileprivate properties are accessible anywhere within the same source file where they are defined.
lesson:
Lesson: Access Control
Access control restricts access to parts of your code from code in other source files and modules.

Why Use Access Control?
1. Encapsulation: Hide implementation details that shouldn't be exposed.
2. Safety: Prevent external code from modifying internal state in unexpected ways.
3. API Design: Clearly define which parts of your code are meant for public use.

Access Levels:
- 'private': The most restrictive. Use it for state that should only be touched by the current struct or class.
- 'internal': The default. Great for modular code within a single project.
- 'public': Use this when building a library or framework.
- 'open': Like public, but also allows subclasses and overrides outside the module.

Example:
    struct Vault {
        private var secretCode: Int
        private(set) var isOpen: Bool = false
        init(code: Int) { self.secretCode = code }
    }

Best Practices:
- Start with the most restrictive level ('private') and only open it up as needed.
- Use 'private(set)' for state that should be visible but not modifiable by others.
title: Access Control Nuance

hints: Add a where clause to limit the default method.
Return a formatted string using tag.
The extension should be 'extension Taggable where Self: CustomStringConvertible { ... }'.
title: Protocol Extension Constraint

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
hints: Memberwise init uses property names as labels.
Print the stored properties in order.
The memberwise initializer is automatically provided by Swift for any struct that doesn't define its own custom initializers.
lesson:
Lesson: Structs
Structs are the building blocks of most Swift programs. They are 'value types', meaning they are copied when passed around.

Core Concepts:
1. Definition: Use the 'struct' keyword. Properties are variables or constants defined inside.
2. Memberwise Initializer: Swift automatically provides an initializer based on your properties.
3. Value Semantics: When you assign a struct to a new variable, you get a copy. Changes to the copy don't affect the original.
4. Mutability: If you want to change a struct's properties, the instance itself must be declared with 'var'.

Example:
    struct ForgeItem {
        var name: String
        var weight: Int
    }
    var item = ForgeItem(name: "Iron", weight: 5)
    item.weight = 10 // Only works because 'item' is var

Best Practices:
- Use structs for simple data models.
- Favor immutability (let) whenever possible.
solution:
let ingot = Ingot(metal: "Iron", weight: 3)
print("\(ingot.metal) \(ingot.weight)")
title: Memberwise Init

cheatsheet:
Classes (Reference Types)
- Definition: class Name { var property: Type; init(...) { ... } }
- Reference Semantics: Multiple variables can point to the same instance
- Identity: Use === to check if two variables point to the same instance
- Initializers: Unlike structs, classes DO NOT get a memberwise initializer automatically
- Deinit: A deinitializer runs when the last reference to an instance is released
- Inheritance: Classes can inherit from other classes (subclassing)
hints: Use the identity operator (===) for classes.
The '===' operator checks if two references point to the exact same object in memory.
The '==' operator (if implemented via Equatable) checks if two objects have the same value.
lesson:
Lesson: Classes
Classes are 'reference types'. When you assign a class instance to a new variable, both variables point to the same object in memory.

Key Differences from Structs:
1. Reference Semantics: Changing a property through one reference affects all other references to that same instance.
2. Mandatory Initializers: You must provide an 'init' method if your properties don't have default values.
3. Identity vs. Equality: Use '==' for value equality and '===' for reference identity.
4. Lifetime: Classes use Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) to determine when they should be destroyed.

Example:
    class Bellows {
        var power: Int
        init(power: Int) { self.power = power }
    }
    let p1 = Bellows(power: 1)
    let p2 = p1
    p2.power = 10 // p1.power is now also 10

When to Use:
- Use classes when you need shared mutable state or identity.
- Use classes for inheritance or when you need to perform cleanup on deinit.
solution: print(primary === secondary)
title: Class Identity

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
hints: Only provide a get block.
Use integer division for 28 / 14.
A read-only computed property doesn't need the 'get' keyword if it only has a getter; you can just provide the body inside the braces.
title: Readonly Property

hints: Default methods live in protocol extensions.
The extension should be 'extension Describable { ... }'.
Types that conform to 'Describable' will inherit this 'describe()' implementation unless they provide their own.
title: Protocol Default

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
hints: Static methods are called on the type.
The 'standard()' function should be defined as 'static func standard() -> Alloy'.
Because it's a factory method, it returns a new instance of the type it's extending.
lesson:
Lesson: Extensions
Extensions add new functionality to an existing type, even if you don't have access to the original source code.

What Extensions Can Do:
1. Add Methods: Group related utility functions outside the main type definition.
2. Computed Properties: Add derived state without adding stored data.
3. Protocol Conformance: Keep your code organized by putting protocol requirements in an extension.
4. Default Implementations: Use protocol extensions to provide 'free' functionality to any type that conforms.

Example:
    extension Int {
        var isEven: Bool { return self % 2 == 0 }
    }
    print(4.isEven) // true

Best Practices:
- Use extensions to break up large types into logical blocks.
- Don't use extensions to hide complexity; use them to organize it.
title: Extension Static

cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
hints: private(set) allows reads but restricts writes.
The 'value' property can be read from outside the 'Counter' struct, but only the 'Counter' struct (via the 'increment' method) can modify it.
This is a common pattern for ensuring internal consistency while allowing external monitoring.
lesson:
Lesson: Access Control
Access control restricts access to parts of your code from code in other source files and modules.

Why Use Access Control?
1. Encapsulation: Hide implementation details that shouldn't be exposed.
2. Safety: Prevent external code from modifying internal state in unexpected ways.
3. API Design: Clearly define which parts of your code are meant for public use.

Access Levels:
- 'private': The most restrictive. Use it for state that should only be touched by the current struct or class.
- 'internal': The default. Great for modular code within a single project.
- 'public': Use this when building a library or framework.
- 'open': Like public, but also allows subclasses and overrides outside the module.

Example:
    struct Vault {
        private var secretCode: Int
        private(set) var isOpen: Bool = false
        init(code: Int) { self.secretCode = code }
    }

Best Practices:
- Start with the most restrictive level ('private') and only open it up as needed.
- Use 'private(set)' for state that should be visible but not modifiable by others.
solution:
var counter = Counter()
counter.increment()
counter.increment()
print(counter.value)
title: Private(set)

cheatsheet:
Error Handling
- Defining Errors: Use an enum that conforms to 'Error'.
- Throwing: Use 'throw MyError.something' inside a function marked 'throws'.
- Handling:
  - do { try function() } catch { ... }
  - try?: Returns an optional, nil if an error was thrown.
  - try!: Disables error propagation; crashes if an error is thrown.
- Rethrows: Used when a function takes a throwing closure and propagates its errors.
hints: Use a specific catch clause for the enum case.
The catch clause should look like 'catch FuseError.blown { ... }'.
You can have multiple catch clauses to handle different error cases specifically.
title: Error Specific

- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Store two values in a generic type
hints: Use two generic type parameters.

Assign the parameters to the properties in the order they are passed.

hints: Use 'any' to spell an existential.
Access heat through the protocol-typed value.
The 'any' keyword is required in modern Swift when using a protocol as a type.
solution:
let source: any HeatSource = Burner(heat: 1500)
print(source.heat)

hints: Combine protocols with '&' and prefix with 'any'.
The type should be 'any Fueling & Venting'.

description: Constrain a generic method with where
hints: Use a where clause on the method, not the type.
The method signature should be 'func allEqual() -> Bool where T: Equatable'.
This allows the method to exist on all 'Store' instances, but it can only be CALLED if 'T' is 'Equatable'.

Because the extension is specific to 'String', you know that 'items.first' will be a 'String' (or nil).

cheatsheet:
Memory Management (ARC)
- ARC: Automatic Reference Counting manages memory for class instances.
- Strong Reference: The default. Keeps the instance alive as long as the reference exists.
- Weak Reference: Does not keep the instance alive. Automatically becomes 'nil' when the instance is released. Must be 'var' and optional.
- Reference Cycle: Occurs when two instances hold strong references to each other, preventing them from ever being released.
- [weak self]: Used in closure capture lists to avoid reference cycles.
hints: Capture weakly to avoid a strong cycle.
Use nil coalescing to print "nil" when missing.
The closure already has '[weak forge]', so 'forge' is an optional inside the closure.
When the original 'forge' is set to 'nil', the weak reference inside the closure also becomes 'nil'.
lesson:
Lesson: Memory Management
Swift uses Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) to track and manage your app's memory usage.

How ARC Works:
1. Every time you create a class instance, ARC allocates a chunk of memory.
2. ARC tracks how many properties, constants, and variables are currently referring to each class instance.
3. When the reference count drops to zero, the instance is deallocated.

Reference Cycles:
Cycles are "memory leaks". They happen most often with:
- Parent-child relationships (e.g., a Department has many Employees, and each Employee knows their Department).
- Closures that capture 'self' while 'self' also holds a reference to the closure.

The Solution:
- Use 'weak' for one side of a parent-child relationship.
- Use '[weak self]' in closures.

Best Practices:
- Most references should be strong. Only use 'weak' when you identify a potential cycle.
- Structs and enums are value types and don't use ARC.
title: Weak Release

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Add a computed property via extension
hints: Extensions can add computed properties.
Return a Bool based on weight.
The property should look like 'var isHeavy: Bool { return weight >= 10 }'.
lesson:
Lesson: Extensions
Extensions add new functionality to an existing type, even if you don't have access to the original source code.

What Extensions Can Do:
1. Add Methods: Group related utility functions outside the main type definition.
2. Computed Properties: Add derived state without adding stored data.
3. Protocol Conformance: Keep your code organized by putting protocol requirements in an extension.
4. Default Implementations: Use protocol extensions to provide 'free' functionality to any type that conforms.

Example:
    extension Int {
        var isEven: Bool { return self % 2 == 0 }
    }
    print(4.isEven) // true

Best Practices:
- Use extensions to break up large types into logical blocks.
- Don't use extensions to hide complexity; use them to organize it.
title: Extension Computed

cheatsheet:
Error Handling
- Defining Errors: Use an enum that conforms to 'Error'.
- Throwing: Use 'throw MyError.something' inside a function marked 'throws'.
- Handling:
  - do { try function() } catch { ... }
  - try?: Returns an optional, nil if an error was thrown.
  - try!: Disables error propagation; crashes if an error is thrown.
- Rethrows: Used when a function takes a throwing closure and propagates its errors.
hints: rethrows forwards errors from the closure.
Handle the error in the catch block.
The 'run' function should use the 'rethrows' keyword because it takes a closure that 'throws'.
Inside 'run', call the closure with 'try' and return its result.
title: Rethrow
